insight: When building Tarsiers into Amporilla in Ebb+Amporilla sets, the timing of Amporilla purchase matters more than total damage output. Buying Amporilla earlier (at 4 Tarsiers for ~4 damage) can create better economic momentum than delaying for higher initial damage, because it accelerates your economy and forces opponent pressure decisions earlier.
author: mrguy888, mqp
context: Debate about optimal Amporilla timing with early Tarsier stacks.

insight: Shadowfang is an economy unit that requires mid-size economy to function effectively. Building multiple Shadowfangs requires sufficient drone base; too low economy cannot pump out multiple Fangs without dying to pressure.
author: liadahlia
context: Discussion of Shadowfang economic requirements in Corpus+Shadowfang set.

insight: Deadeye Operative requires a 4 Engineer base minimum for optimal play. The standard approach is to purchase Deadeyes while maintaining 2 Engineers, then continue buying additional Engineers and Drones after commitment, rather than stopping at 2 Engineers.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion of Deadeye Operative economy and Engineer count requirements.

insight: As Player 2 in Deadeye sets, the standard 3rd Engineer may be skipped in favor of Drone continuation because P2 risks running out of Drone supply while P1 sacrifices tempo with 3rd Engineer.
author: Deleted User, p0lari
context: P2 optimization in Deadeye sets when Drone supply is limited.

insight: Against Shredder pressure, optimal priority is: (1) kill enemy Shredder, (2) click enemy Auride if able, (3) hold Drones until defended, (4) buy as many Hellhound as possible. This maximizes attack value by using it to clear frontline units and generate gold.
author: apooche
context: Tactical priority order for managing Shredder-heavy sets.

insight: Frostbrooder is countered hard by Xeno Guardian. If opponent builds Xenos to counter Brooders, you at least inconvenience their economy, but Tarsiers are generally preferable to Brooders if available.
author: amalloy, Deleted User
context: Matchup analysis of Frostbrooder vs Xeno Guardian in Centurion sets.

insight: Zemora Voidbringer is primarily a slow value unit. Success with Zemora requires getting it into play and reaching sufficient Conduits to power it without dying to pressure. Red color with Zemora is viable if other defensive red units exist (like Cauterizer).
author: mqp, p0lari
context: Analysis of Zemora viability with red-heavy unit sets.

insight: When switching from Tarsier to Rhino in red rush defense, the ideal timing is when you need to purchase a prompt Rhino for the following turn. However, Tarsier is generally better value, so err on the side of switching late. Sets with Husk may not require Rhino trains at all.
author: 307th
context: Guidance on Tarsier-to-Rhino transition timing in defensive sequences.

insight: Energy Matrix is a powerful absorber worth building additional tech (like a 2nd Blastforge) to access, even if it causes color waste. Energy Matrix effectively generates 2 free Engineers per turn by doubling wall defense value, justifying high economy builds.
author: 307th, Deleted User
context: Analysis of Energy Matrix's defensive value and justification for economic investment.

insight: Iceblade is weak against Energy Matrix+Walls combination because Iceblade has odd health, making it difficult to efficiently damage through the absorber-wall defense.
author: darkeisbein
context: Unit matchup analysis in defensive scenarios.

insight: Fission Turret as a first attacker can work in rush scenarios, but early Fission Turrets will be absorbed with low value because of their lifespan mechanic. Building them requires confidence in spending accumulated gold efficiently.
author: 307th, darkeisbein
context: Discussion of Fission Turret timing and viability as opening attackers.

insight: Trinity Drone enables lower-commitment access to Fission Turrets. Tricolor builds with Trinity Drone and Energy Matrix allow flexible transition into Fission Turrets while maintaining economy.
author: .bky_1556
context: Tricolor opening theory with Trinity Drone and Energy Matrix synergy.

insight: In breachproof mirror situations with Gauss Cannon, killing your opponent's Gauss Cannons removes their options if the damage can't secure a full kill anyway. This is more valuable than splitting damage across units.
author: extratricky
context: Tactical prioritization in mirror breachproof endgames.

insight: Base Set + Venge Cannon is highly solved favoring Player 2. P2 should go natural Conduit (not turn 1 DC). P1 opens with natural Blastforge into Splitter, then Conduit. The matchup is heavily explored with consensus that nothing beats this line.
author: steel0229e, apooche, mrguy888, mtanzer, spiritfryer
context: Analysis of Base+Venge Cannon opening theory after competitive testing.

insight: Base Set + Venge Cannon P2 strategy: get Conduits, multiply economic production by 3 (via Venge Cannon multiplier), and match opponent numbers. This multiplication principle is core to the P2 gameplan.
author: mrguy888
context: Fundamental Base+Venge strategy explanation.

insight: Base Set + Venge Cannon: DD/DDEE opening as P1 is superior to 1EE/1DD when planning turn 3 Fuge and turn 4 Animus, because it avoids wasting energy on the Animus purchase turn. Both lines reach 16 effective drones, but DD/DDEE maintains a Vivid instead of ending with only 1 Drone.
author: apooche
context: Detailed opening line comparison from VOD review.

insight: Holding a Steelsplitter to buy another Steelsplitter is rarely correct; clicking the held Steelsplitter and buying a Wall instead generates more value, except when Wall supply is short.
author: mqp
context: Mechanical optimization for unit clicking priority.

insight: At 1600 MMR, most improvement comes from fixing mechanical errors (overteching, wasting energy in opening) rather than set reading. Watching high-level streams and replays becomes more valuable at 1700-1800+ when set reading is the limiting factor.
author: 307th
context: Skill development roadmap for intermediate players.

insight: Preemptive defense timing: anticipate when opponent will commit to a strong unit (like Odin) and prepare defensive infrastructure before that turn, rather than reacting after the unit appears.
author: itsotter
context: Discussion of defensive preparation timing.

insight: In asymmetric breachproof endgames, the rule of thumb for target priority is: (1) kill permanent things first if possible, (2) if not, save significant units if you can, (3) if neither applies, the situation likely transposes equally. In symmetric situations, killing one-use damages like Pixie or Gauss Charge depends on whether it enables a full kill.
author: redrame, extratricky
context: Breachproof combat prioritization guidelines.
